In the evaluation of tumor proliferation as a prognostic indicator, the Ki67 cut-off percentages serve as a pivotal metric in
distinguishing between varying degrees of tumor aggressiveness. As illustrated in Table 1, a cut-off of 10% for Ki67 shows a
statistically significant association with median overall survival (OS) outcomes, with a p-value of less than 0.001. This suggests
that higher Ki67 levels may correlate with poorer prognosis in patients. The hazard ratio (HR) of 2.953 indicates that patients
with a Ki67 score above this threshold are nearly three times more likely to experience diminished survival compared to those
with lower scores. Furthermore, the accompanying 95% confidence interval (CI) of 1.967 to 4.432 reinforces the robustness of
this association. These findings are critical for clinicians in stratifying patients based on tumor biology and in guiding treatment
decisions. Additional patient metrics, such as weight loss percentage and stool consistency, can further contextualize the clinical
implications of Ki67 levels, enhancing the overall assessment of patient health status and disease progression.
